title: Insights into the electrorheological and electrohydrodynamic regimes in electrically driven emulsion
abstract: Recently, we reported the electrorheoimaging (ERI) technique (Bahraminasr et al, 2026), and found that frequency-dependent electric field of an oil-in-oil emulsion yields two distinct regimes: a high-frequency dipolar, electrorheological (ER) regime and a low-frequency electrohydrodynamic (EHD) regime. In this work, we identify a phenomenological model to fit the results in the ER regime to a classic yield-stress fluid, and find collapse onto a master curve upon rescaling, consistent with a yield stress that grows approximately as $E^2$. Macroscopic small-amplitude oscillatory shear (SAOS) rheology is compared with passive microrheology employing differential dynamic microscopy (DDM), with the close agreement implying scale independence of the ER behaviour, and indicating that, unlike steady shear, SAOS measurements do not restructure these samples and probe underlying material properties. Finally, under the presence of both steady shear and electric fields in the EHD regime, the emulsion forms banded structures composed of alternating droplet-rich and droplet-depleted regions. We explore recurrence and divergence in the location of these bands: they emerge within seconds of field application and decay rapidly after the field is switched off. Using the Jensen--Shannon divergence between radial intensity profiles, we show that the driven structure loses memory on timescales of order $1~s$ commensurate with the timescale of the EHD convection roll. For much longer field-off intervals successive banding events become statistically independent.
summary: This paper explores how electric fields drive an emulsion into two distinct regimes: a predictable electrorheological state and a complex, banded electrohydrodynamic state. It demonstrates that the system's structural memory is tied to the lifetime of underlying convective flows. The work provides a bridge between microscopic droplet dynamics and macroscopic rheological properties.
main_problem: Investigating the transition between electrorheological (ER) and electrohydrodynamic (EHD) regimes in electrically driven emulsions and quantifying the structural memory and decorrelation timescales in the EHD regime.
main_result: The ER regime behaves as a yield-stress fluid with a yield stress scaling as E^2, while the EHD regime exhibits banded structures that lose memory on a timescale of ~1s, matching the electroconvective roll turnover time.
method: The study combines macroscopic rheology (SAOS and steady shear) with microscopic microrheology (Differential Dynamic Microscopy) and electrorheoimaging (ERI) using Jensen-Shannon divergence for structural analysis.
model_or_system: An oil-in-oil emulsion (castor oil droplets in motor oil) subjected to frequency-dependent electric fields and controlled shear rates within a vertical field sample cell.
key_observables: Complex shear modulus (G', G''), yield stress (sigma_y), mean-squared displacement (MSD), radial intensity profiles, and Jensen-Shannon divergence.
important_parameters: Electric field strength (E_rms), driving frequency (f), shear rate (gamma_dot), and field-off interval (tau_off).
main_assumptions: The ER regime can be modeled as a classic yield-stress fluid using the generalized Herschel-Bulkley model.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Experimental setup and ERI imaging; Fig 2: Flow curves and master curve data collapse; Fig 5: Evolution of intensity profiles and structural decorrelation; Fig 6: Comparison of macroscopic and microscopic viscoelastic moduli.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the ERI technique, characterizes the high-frequency ER regime via scaling and model fitting, compares macro- and microrheology, analyzes the low-frequency EHD regime's banding and memory effects, and concludes with implications for driven soft matter.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental Setup (a) Schematic of the vertical field sam- ple cell consisting of two parallel indium tin oxide (ITO)–coated glass coverslips separated by a 270µm spacer. (b) Schematic of electro-rheo-imaging (ERI) setup. Electrical contact is made with the high-voltage wire (red and blue) the sample illuminated from the side. Imaging is performed from the bottom with a camera. (c) A sample image of bright droplets against dark background.

FIG. 2. Frequency-dependent flow curves: curve-fitting and data collapse. (a) Flow curves measured at fixed root-mean-square electric field, Erms = 3.7 V/µm, for frequencies between 50 and 500 Hz, fitted using gHB model in Eq. 7. The fitted shear-thinning exponent remains close to n = 1 (b) Master-curve representation of the data in (a), obtained by plotting the reduced shear stress (σ1/2 −σ1/2 y )/σ1/2 y )2 as a function of the rescaled shear rate k ˙γn

FIG. 3. Field-amplitude-dependent flow curves: curve-fitting and data collapse. (a) Flow curves measured at fixed frequency, 500 Hz, for different electric field strengths, showing that the Casson limit (n = 1) provides a good description over the full range of applied fields. (b) The corresponding rescaled data collapse onto a single master curve. (c) Measured normal force as a function of E2rms. The approximately linear dependence is consistent with the capacitor scaling FN = ¯κε0AE2/2, and the fitted effective dielectric constant is close to the expected value for the emulsion. FIG. 4. Failure of collapse in the EHD regime: (a) Flow curves showing non-monotonic evolution of the shear stress σ with decreas- ing frequency in the EHD-dominated regime. Curves are shifted downward from 40 Hz to ∼2 Hz, and then upwards from 2 Hz to DC. (b,c) Shear stress at fixed shear rates, (b) ¯˙γ = 1.14 s−1 and (c) 528 s−1, highlight the contrasting behavior: strong frequency depen- dence at low shear (EHD-dominated) and convergence of all curves at high shear where hydrodynamic forcing dominates.

FIG. 5. Band identification via radial intensity analysis: (a) The full field of view of aand the extracted 1000×50 pixel strip used for analysis. (b) One-dimensional radial intensity profile I(r,t) obtained by averaging a 10-pixel-wide band, together with the smoothed background profile Ibg (200-pixel moving average). (c) Fluctuation signal ∆I(r,t) = I(r,t)−Ibg, where local maxima identify droplet-rich bands and local minima correspond to depletion regions. Recurrence as a function of the τoff timescale: Analysis of band formation and relaxation under intermittent electric field.
